chunk_summary: Guidelines for complementary feeding in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants emphasize protecting milk intake, structured meal progression, and nutrient-dense first foods to support growth and development.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of using corrected age and developmental readiness for starting complementary feeding in preterm infants.
Learn structured meal-frequency progression to protect milk intake and ensure adequate energy and nutrient supply.
Recognize the role of iron-dense first foods in meeting higher iron needs of preterm infants.
Identify signs of feeding intolerance and growth faltering to adjust feeding practices.
key_facts: Complementary feeding often starts around 6 months corrected age for very preterm infants.
Meal progression: 6–8 months corrected age, 2–3 small meals/day; 9–11 months corrected age, 3–4 meals/day plus 1–2 snacks; by 12 months corrected age, transition to family meal patterns.
Milk should remain the dominant nutrition source during early complementary feeding stages.
Iron-dense first foods like egg, meat/fish puree, or lentils are recommended due to higher iron needs in preterm infants.
Reassess growth and feeding tolerance 2–4 weeks after starting solids, or sooner if symptoms like diarrhea, vomiting, or growth faltering occur.
reference_quote: Complementary feeding in former preterm infants should add nutrient density and oral skills without displacing milk calories too early. Use corrected age and developmental readiness for the start date, then use a structured meal-frequency progression to protect total energy intake. (WHO, Reference 1)
document_key_topics: Complementary feeding protocols for preterm infants
Meal frequency progression and milk intake protection
Iron-dense first foods for preterm infants
